original_title: Magnetic Stirring-Driven Synthesis of Neutral Donor–Acceptor [2] and [3]Rotaxanes under Minimal Solvent Conditions
title: Rotaxanes made by just stirring harder, barely any solvent required
blurb: Neutral donor-acceptor [2]- and [3]rotaxanes assembled under near solvent-free conditions, driven by nothing more exotic than magnetic stirring. If it scales past a stir bar's worth of material, it's a genuinely cheap route to mechanically interlocked molecules.

title: Terpene synthase chemistry, but a small-molecule catalyst does the asymmetric part
blurb: A catalytic system runs cationic cyclizations on geranyl substrates enantioselectively, the same kind of polyene cyclization terpene synthases handle enzymatically, now under synthetic asymmetric control.
venue: Journal of the American Chemical Society

original_title: Decoupled Molecular Motifs and Supramolecular Dimers for Highly Efficient FDCA Synthesis from Neutral Biomass
title: A biomass-to-FDCA catalyst built by splitting the grabbing job from the reacting job
blurb: Separates the molecular motifs that bind the substrate from the ones that do the actual chemistry, then lets them pair up as supramolecular dimers, pushing FDCA synthesis from untreated biomass to high efficiency. FDCA is the monomer everyone wants for bio-based PET, so a cleaner route straight from raw biomass actually matters.
venue: Journal of the American Chemical Society

original_title: Metal-Free C−H Aroylation of Alkenes Mediated by Triarylamine Visible-Light Photocatalysis
title: Visible light and an amine catalyst do the aroylation, no metal in sight
blurb: Triarylamine photocatalysis puts an aroyl group onto alkenes under visible light with no metal catalyst involved. One less metal to strip back out of the product later.

original_title: Aldehyde/Triazolium-Catalyzed Transformation of C(sp 3 )–H Bonds via HAT Photocatalysis and Electron-Shuttle Process: Unexplored Merging of Aldehyde and Triazolium
title: Getting one catalyst to run both the photochemistry and the C–H activation
blurb: Pairs an aldehyde with a triazolium salt so the same system does hydrogen-atom transfer and shuttles the electron, activating C(sp3)-H bonds without a separate photocatalyst. The pitch is that aldehyde and triazolium catalysis hadn't been merged like this before.

original_title: Multifunctional Catechol-Functionalized Cellulose Hydrogels for the Minimally Invasive Treatment of Acute Optic Nerve Injuries.
title: A catechol hydrogel for optic nerve injuries that skips open surgery
blurb: Catechol groups grafted onto a cellulose hydrogel, built as a minimally invasive fix for acute optic nerve injury, where surgical access to the nerve is usually the hard part.

original_title: Visible-Light Photoredox-Catalyzed Radical Addition/Double Cyclization of Indole with Alkenes: Access to gem-Difluorotetrahydrocarbazolones
title: Photoredox builds gem-difluoro tetrahydrocarbazolones in one pot
blurb: A visible-light photoredox radical addition cascades straight into a double cyclization, stitching indoles and alkenes into gem-difluorotetrahydrocarbazolones in a single step instead of doing the ring closures separately.

original_title: Copper-Catalyzed Cross-Coupling of Malonates with Aryl Halides Using Hydroxypicolinamide Ligands
title: A copper ligand aimed at making malonate arylation scale-friendly
blurb: Cu-catalyzed coupling of malonates with aryl halides using hydroxypicolinamide ligands in place of the usual diamine or phenanthroline setups. Showing up in Organic Process Research & Development is the tell that this is aimed at running at scale, not just filling out a substrate table.

original_title: Cu-Catalyzed Regioselective and Enantioselective Allylation of Ketones with Allenylsilanes: Mechanistic Insights and Selectivity Origins via Density Functional Theory Analysis
title: DFT explains why this copper catalyst picks one allylation site over another
blurb: Cu-catalyzed allylation of ketones with allenylsilanes that comes out both regio- and enantioselective, with DFT brought in to explain where that selectivity actually comes from rather than just reporting the numbers.

original_title: Native Multidimensional Protein Complex Identification and Topology Characterization (Native MudPIT)
title: MudPIT gets a native-conditions upgrade for mapping complex topology
blurb: Extends multidimensional protein complex identification (MudPIT) to native conditions, going after both complex identity and topology in one workflow instead of needing a separate structural method for the second part.

original_title: Photoinduced C3 - Alkylation of Pyridines with Unactivated Alkyl Halides
title: Alkylating pyridines at C3 without needing an activated alkyl halide
blurb: Photoinduced C3-alkylation of pyridines that works with unactivated alkyl halides, the kind that normally need extra activation to react at all. Opens direct pyridine functionalization to a much wider pool of halides.

title: How to Handle Selenium Dioxide
blurb: I was doing a Riley oxidation in the lab, and made sure to follow what I understand is best protocol. I did everything in the hood--weighing, setting up the reaction, and washed the spatula into a selenium waste container. However, taking the spatula out of the hood produced a smell and I put it back as soon as this was recognized. Did I do something incorrectly? Do I need to be concerned about exposure?

title: Palladium (II) bromide reaction with microspatula
blurb: After a long wait, the PdBr2 we ordered came in the mail and I got right to it. After weighing out samples for a few reactions I wiped it clean and found the parts of my microspatula that came into contact with the PdBr2 turned cloudy/white. Nothing I did removed it, so I'm pretty sure it's embedded. I don't know what metal plating this is exactly. The thing is that I've used PdCl2 plenty with this microspatula and there was no issue, so what's going on here?
